---
title: Ending Design
tags: [endings, structure]
---

# Ending Design

An ending is the payoff for every choice a reader made on the way to
it, so it must visibly answer the story's central dilemma rather than
simply stopping. A branching gamebook usually needs several endings —
one per hard fork's terminal state — but that is not license to write
endings as an afterthought: each one is the destination the whole path
was walking toward.

## Salience

Not every ending carries equal weight. A quiet, bittersweet ending that
answers the story's real question can matter more than a loud one that
answers nothing. Rank endings by how directly they resolve the central
dilemma, not by how much spectacle they contain.

## Avoiding the false ending

A false ending — a scene that reads as final but is actually a
mid-story beat — breaks trust with the reader. Reserve ending-shaped
prose (a summarizing final line, a fade-to-black) for beats that are
actually terminal in the graph.
